title: People
text:
Jonas Cremer


Principal Investigator


January 2020 – present




About


Jonas Cremer is an Assistant Professor in Biology. He is interested in the physiology and growth of prokaryotes. Jonas studied physics and biophysics in Munich. He was a postdoctoral researcher at the University of California, San Diego. Before joining Stanford, he was an Assistant Professor at the University of Groningen. His current research focuses on gut bacteria and the model organism Escherichia coli.






Théo Gervais


Postdoctoral Scholar


January 2025 – present




About


Théo Gervais is a Postdoctoral Scholar jointly advised by Jonas Cremer and KC Huang. He obtained his PhD from the Biozentrum of Basel University studying quantitative microbial physiology using microfluidics and fluorescent microscopy to study E. coli carbon starvation at the single-cell level.






Mathis Leblanc


PhD Student in Biophysics


July 2022 – present




About


Mathis Leblanc is a graduate student in Biophysics. Trained in protein thermodynamics, Mathis is now interested in the intersection of molecular biophysics and bacterial physiology. He uses numerical simulations to investigate cell-shape regulation in Escherichia coli. Mathis received his B.A. in Biophysics at Johns Hopkins in 2021.






Shaili Mathur


PhD Student in Biology


June 2022 – present




About


Shaili Mathur (she/her) is a PhD student in Biology in the Ecology and Evolution Track, jointly advised by Jonas Cremer and Dmitri Petrov. She combines mathematical, computational, and experimental techniques to study complex biological systems in dynamic environments. Shaili received her B.S. in Computational and Systems Biology and M.S. in Bioinformatics from UCLA in 2021.






Leron Perez


PhD Candidate in Biophysics


July 2021 – present




About


Leron Perez (he/him) is a PhD student in Biophysics, jointly advised by Jonas Cremer and Drew Endy. He is interested in understanding microbial adaptation to uncertain and changing environments to improve synthetic biological design. Leron received his B.S. in Molecular Engineering at the University of Chicago in 2020.






Richa Sharma


Postdoctoral Scholar


January 2021 – present




About


Richa Sharma is a Postdoctoral Research Fellow interested in the eco-physiology of human gut strains including Escherichia coli and beyond. She aims to understand the functional aspects of resident gut strains and their impact on human health. She attained her doctoral degree in Soil Microbial Ecology from IIT Delhi in November 2019.

title: Join the Lab
text:
Postdoctoral Scholar — Quantitative Flux Analyses in the Human Gut



We are looking for a postdoc to lead projects on quantitative flux analyses in the human gut microbiota. The position combines in-vitro experimentation (HPLC, bioreactors, anaerobic culturing) with mathematical modeling to understand how gut bacteria metabolize substrates and produce fermentation products. Strong quantitative background and interest in microbial physiology or metabolism required.
Contact jonas.cremer@stanford.edu with a CV and brief statement of interest.








Postdoctoral Scholar — Physiological Principles of Growth



We are looking for a postdoc to work on the physiological principles underlying bacterial growth, with a focus on E. coli as a model organism. Projects combine quantitative experiments (growth physiology, proteomics, microscopy) with mathematical modeling. Experience in microbiology, quantitative biology, or biophysics is welcome.
Contact jonas.cremer@stanford.edu with a CV and brief statement of interest.

title: Join the Lab
text:
Graduate Students
If you are currently enrolled at Stanford and interested in doing a rotation in our lab, please contact Jonas to schedule a meeting. We love to hear about your scientific ideas and visions.
We have experimental projects available in microbiology and microbiota research — ranging from fundamental questions of bacterial physiology to more biomedical questions. Experimentally, we focus on the quantitative culturing of bacteria, integrating bioreactors, microscopy, HPLC, and mathematical modeling.
Not at Stanford yet? Consider applying to the Biology graduate program, the Biophysics program, or any other Bio Sciences graduate program at Stanford.

title: Join the Lab
text:
Undergraduate Students
We participate in the Bio-X SURF Program and the B-SURP Program. Feel free to contact Jonas via email with information about your background and interests.

title: Join the Lab
text:
Lab Community
To learn more about our commitment to building an inclusive and collaborative lab environment, please read our lab values and community guidelines.

title: Contact
text:
Lab Guidelines & Community
We are committed to building an open, inclusive, and collaborative lab environment. Our lab community statement and guidelines can be found here.

title: Research
text: Admittedly, we are a bit obsessed with microbial growth. How do tiny microbial cells harbor thousands of enzymes and coordinate a myriad of processes to replicate and thrive? Can we find in a sea of complexity the essential principles which determine growth? We approach these fundamental questions of systems biology by tightly combining experiments across conditions with mathematical modeling and data analysis — mostly for Escherichia coli, this wonderfully studied model organism, but increasingly also for other microbes across biological domains.

title: Research
text: Admittedly, we are a bit obsessed with microbial growth. How do tiny microbial cells harbor thousands of enzymes and coordinate a myriad of processes to replicate and thrive? Can we find in a sea of complexity the essential principles which determine growth? We approach these fundamental questions of systems biology by tightly combining experiments across conditions with mathematical modeling and data analysis — mostly for Escherichia coli, this wonderfully studied model organism, but increasingly also for other microbes across biological domains.

title: Research
section: Microbial life at the triangle of physiology, ecology, and evolution
text:
Microbial life at the triangle of physiology, ecology, and evolution
Our quantitative investigations also go well beyond the cellular level. Integrating ecological and evolutionary considerations, we explore how cell physiological constraints determine adaptation to specific environments and habitats.



The triangle of microbial life

title: Research
text:
The human gut microbiota
We further build on our cell-physiological understanding to better rationalize the composition of the human gut microbiota and its effect on host health. Towards a more mechanistic understanding, we follow quantitative approaches that combine in-vitro experimentation and mathematical modeling to link considerations of cell metabolism, microbial population dynamics, and intestinal physiology.

title: Courses
text: Current and previous teaching activities can also be found on the Stanford ExploreCourses website.

title: Courses
section: BIO165/265 — Quantitative Cell Biology: From Molecules to Evolution
text:
BIO165/265 — Quantitative Cell Biology: From Molecules to Evolution
Winter Quarter · Undergraduate (BIO165) and Graduate (BIO265)
A combination of classical lectures and Python-based dry-lab sessions exploring how major cellular processes must be orchestrated and fine-tuned within the cellular environment to form a functional unit. Students analyze recently collected experimental data and are introduced to modern mathematical modeling approaches. Designed for students from biology and related disciplines interested in quantitative approaches to cell biology.
→ Course website · → ExploreCourses

title: Courses
text:
BIO120/220 — Integrative and Experimental Microbiology
Spring Quarter · Undergraduate (BIO120) and Graduate (BIO220)
A hands-on lab course in microbiology co-taught with Sharon Long. Students gain practical experience with core microbiology techniques alongside an integrative understanding of microbial physiology and growth. The course combines experimental work with quantitative analysis.
→ ExploreCourses

title: Lab Community
text: Science can be a truly inspiring process, particularly when working together in a diverse team. This document was put together jointly by Jonas and many lab members (last updated August 2023).

title: Lab Community
section: We foster an inclusive research environment
text:
We foster an inclusive research environment
We are committed to building a lab that is welcoming to all, regardless of ethnic, socioeconomic, gender, or sexual orientation background. We acknowledge the systemic failures that exist in academia and call on each lab member to actively help combat microaggressions and bias in our day-to-day work. Reports of harassment can be directed to the biology department’s reporting channels, and Jonas will always be available to discuss concerns directly.

title: Lab Community
section: We keep life and work in a healthy balance
text:
We keep life and work in a healthy balance
The health and wellbeing of lab members is our top priority. There is no expectation of evening or weekend work, nor of prompt replies to Slack or email outside of working hours. If you ever feel overwhelmed — by workload, personal circumstances, or anything else — please speak with Jonas. We want everyone to thrive both inside and outside the lab.

title: Lab Community
section: We respect the intellectual work of other lab members
text:
We respect the intellectual work of other lab members
Open exchange of ideas is one of the great joys of science. Collaborative projects require active, ongoing discussion around contributions, authorship, and attribution. We make these conversations explicit and early rather than leaving them for later.

title: Lab Community
section: We discuss openly but always respect our co-scientists
text:
We discuss openly but always respect our co-scientists
Ideas should be freely raised and challenged on scientific grounds. We maintain a culture of intellectual honesty and directness while always treating colleagues with respect, regardless of experience level or place in the lab hierarchy.

title: Lab Community
text:
Safety first
The lab operates at BSL-2. All safety procedures must be followed at all times. Communicate any doubts or concerns about safety practices to Jonas or the lab safety officer — there is no such thing as a trivial safety question.

title: Lab Community
section: We jointly contribute to keeping the lab well-maintained
text:
We jointly contribute to keeping the lab well-maintained
Everyone shares responsibility for chemicals, consumables, equipment, and waste. Participation in at least one annual lab cleaning day is expected from all members.

title: Lab Community
section: We respect each other’s experimental workspace
text:
We respect each other’s experimental workspace
A strict rule: personal bench space, chemical stocks, and pipettes must not be used without prior permission from the owner. When in doubt, ask.

title: Lab Community
text:
We promote open science
Manuscripts and data should be made openly available wherever possible — via preprint servers, GitHub, and data repositories. Raw data storage approaches are documented on the lab Wiki. Openness and reproducibility are values, not afterthoughts.

title: Cremer Lab
text:
Laboratory of Quantitative Microbial Physiology
  
    We combine experimentation, data analysis, and mathematical modeling to
    understand how microbial cells grow, adapt, and shape their environments —
    from individual E. coli to the human gut microbiota.
    Jonas Cremer lab · Stanford University · Department of Biology
  
  
    Our Research
    Meet the Team
    Publications

title: Cremer Lab
text:
Recent News

2025-02 New paper in Nature Communications: Maintenance of cytoplasmic and membrane densities shapes cellular geometry in E. coli. Read paper →


2025-01 PRX Life paper on diurnal variations in gut microbial dynamics featured in a Physics Viewpoint by Anne-Florence Bitbol. Read viewpoint →


2025-01 Welcome to Théo Gervais! New postdoctoral scholar jointly advised with KC Huang.


2024-06 We are hiring! Open postdoc positions in quantitative flux analyses and physiological principles of growth. See open positions →

title: Cremer Lab
text:
Research Themes
Microbial cell physiology How do cells coordinate growth across thousands of processes? We study the essential principles of microbial physiology in E. coli.
Physiology–ecology–evolution How do cell-physiological constraints shape adaptation and evolution in complex environments?
Human gut microbiota How do microbial physiology and intestinal conditions jointly determine gut microbiome composition and function?
→ Learn more about our research
